coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent porous organic cage with good water solubility for ratiometric sensing of gold(III) ion in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Cong
Gu, Biao
Tang, Si-Ping
Deng, Pei-Hong
Liu, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent porous organic cage with good water solubility is of great interest but still challenging for its fluorescent sensing application. Poor water solubility and single signal of most previous probes are unfavorable for the monitoring of Au3+ generated from the potential dissociation of gold nanoparticles in environmental and biological samples. Here we report a water-soluble porous organic cage as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Au3+ in aqueous solution. The prepared porous organic cage with good water solubility showed specific redox interaction with Au3+ in pH 5, leading to the change of dual emission at 420 and 484 nm. Based on the change of fluorescence ratio, a simple ratiometric sensing method for Au3+ from the dissociation of gold nanoparticles in aqueous solution was developed. The proposed method gave a calibration function of F484/F420 = 0.0370[Au3+] + 0.5689 (where F484/F420 is the intensity ratio of fluorescence at 484 nm to that at 420 nm; [Au3+] in μM) (R2 = 0.9975) in the concentration range of 1–60 μM, the limit of detection (3s) of 8 nM, and the relative standard deviation of 0.26% for 10 replicate detections of 50 μM Au3+. The recoveries of spiked Au3+ in domestic wastewater and human serum samples ranged from 94.66% to 105.61%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing
Water solubility
Porous organic cage
Gold(III) ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of reduced porous organic cage (RCC19R)
Ratiometric fluorescent detection of Au3+ in aqueous solution
Collection, pretreatment, and analysis of environmental and biological water samples
The interaction between RCC19R and Au3+
Practical application in environmental and biological water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Youth Program of Nature Science Foundation of Hunan Province [grant number 2020JJ5008 ]; A Project Supported by Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department [grant number 20C0298 ]; and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 21907026 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra of CC19R and RCC19R. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra and (C) fluorescence spectra of RCC19R before and after the incubation with Au3+. (D) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm of RCC19R.

(A) TEM image of the red solution containing AuNPs after the interaction between RCC19R and Au3+. Inset: The lattice fringes of the AuNPs. (B) XPS Au 4f spectra of the AuNPs. (C) FTIR spectra of the AuNPs separated from the reaction mixture and OXRCC19R eluted from the AuNPs.
The three stages of the interaction between RCC19R and Au3+ in pH 5.

[Au3+] dependent fluorescence spectra of RCC19R in the concentration range of (A) 0–60 μM and (B) 60–200 μM ([RCC19R], 20.75 μM; pH, 5; incubation time, 20 min; PBS, 10 mM). (C) Effect of [AA] on F484/F420 of RCC19R incubated with 50 μM Au3+ ([RCC19R], 20.75 μM; pH, 5; incubation time, 20 min; PBS, 10 mM).

(A) Plot of F484/F420 of RCC19R as a function of [Au3+] ([RCC19R], 20.75 μM; pH, 5; incubation time, 20 min; PBS, 10 mM). (B) Effect of incubation time on F484/F420 of RCC19R incubated with Au3+ ([RCC19R], 20.75 μM; pH, 5; PBS, 10 mM). (C) Effect of pH on F484/F420 of RCC19R incubated with or without Au3+ ([RCC19R], 20.75 μM; [Au3+], 50 μM; incubation time, 20 min; PBS, 10 mM). (D) Effect of 500 μM various coexisting ions pretreated without or with 500 μM EDTA on the normalized F484/F420 for 50 μM Au3+ ([RCC19R], 20.75 μM; pH, 5; incubation time, 20 min; PBS, 10 mM).
Schematic illustration for (A) the preparation of RCC19R and (B) the ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Au3+ in aqueous solution.
Analytical results (mean ± s, n = 3) for the determination of Au3+ in domestic wastewater and human serum samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Spiked Au3+ of original wastewater and human serum samples is 40 μM and 2 mM, respectively.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent porous organic cage with good water solubility for ratiometric sensing of gold(III) ion in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hunan Engineering Research Center for Monitoring and Treatment of Heavy Metals Pollution in the Upper Reaches of Xiangjiang River, Hengyang Normal University, Hengyang, 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science
Hunan Engineering Research Center for Monitoring and Treatment of Heavy Metals Pollution in the Upper Reaches of Xiangjiang River

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hunan Engineering Research Center for Monitoring and Treatment of Heavy Metals Pollution in the Upper Reaches of Xiangjiang River, Hengyang Normal University, Hengyang, 421001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent porous organic cage with good water solubility is of great interest but still challenging for its fluorescent sensing application. Poor water solubility and single signal of most previous probes are unfavorable for the monitoring of Au3+ generated from the potential dissociation of gold nanoparticles in environmental and biological samples. Here we report a water-soluble porous organic cage as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Au3+ in aqueous solution. The prepared porous organic cage with good water solubility showed specific redox interaction with Au3+ in pH 5, leading to the change of dual emission at 420 and 484 nm. Based on the change of fluorescence ratio, a simple ratiometric sensing method for Au3+ from the dissociation of gold nanoparticles in aqueous solution was developed. The proposed method gave a calibration function of F484/F420 = 0.0370[Au3+] + 0.5689 (where F484/F420 is the intensity ratio of fluorescence at 484 nm to that at 420 nm; [Au3+] in μM) (R2 = 0.9975) in the concentration range of 1–60 μM, the limit of detection (3s) of 8 nM, and the relative standard deviation of 0.26% for 10 replicate detections of 50 μM Au3+. The recoveries of spiked Au3+ in domestic wastewater and human serum samples ranged from 94.66% to 105.61%.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the window-to-window packing pattern, the imine-based porous organic cage possesses particular porosity, organic solvent solution processability, and chirality [1–4]. Thus, porous organic cage has been applied in various fields, such as gas separation [5,6], enantio-separation and sensing [7,8], isotope separation [9], and nanoparticle protection [10–12]. Besides, on account of the benzene and imine fluorophore in molecular structure, porous organic cage also has great potential in fluorescent sensing, such as copper ion sensing and chirality recognition [13,14]. However, the fluorescence sensing application of porous organic cage is still scarce for its large size and poor water solubility. To decrease the size of porous organic cage, a homochiral solution mixing method and a rapid room-temperature recrystallization method have been developed, widening the potential application in sensing [13,15]. Nonetheless, the remaining precipitation tendency of nanometer-sized porous organic cage can still hinder the fluorescence measurement in aqueous solution, which can be eliminated by water-soluble probe [13]. Therefore, the improvement of the water solubility of porous organic cage is highly desirable to broaden its sensing application in aqueous solution furtherly.

Gold nanoparticles have been widely used for sensing and imaging recently [16,17]. But the ligand-Au+-Au0 structure in gold nanoparticles protected by small molecules, such as citric acid, would be destroyed effortlessly by ions or biomolecules in water samples to generate Au+, owing to the weak coordination ability of small molecule ligands [16–19]. The dissociative Au3+ from the disproportionation or oxidation of Au+ furtherly in aqueous solution can lead to environmental and biological problems, such as the damage of kidney, liver, and peripheral nervous system [20–23]. Therefore, it is vital to develop a simple and sensitive method for the detection of potential dissociative Au3+ in environmental water and biological fluids after the incubation with gold nanoparticles. Compared to traditional techniques, such as atom absorption and emission spectrometry, various inexpensive small molecule fluorescent probes have been developed to improve the performance of Au3+ sensing [23–29]. But due to poor water solubility of organic small molecule probes, most of the fluorescent sensing was proceeded in homogeneous assay system of water and organic solvent, such as H2O/EtOH and H2O/DMSO [23–29]. To achieve the fluorescent sensing of Au3+ in aqueous solution, water soluble probes were developed. For example, guanidinium and N-acetyl-cysteamine functionalized rhodamine with single fluorescence signal were reported recently for Au3+ sensing in aqueous solution [30,31]. However, the accuracy of single signal-based probe may easily be affected by many factors, such as collection efficiency, which can be alleviated by ratiometric probe [32–35]. Thus, the development of ratiometric fluorescent probe with good water solubility for Au3+ sensing in aqueous solution is of great significance.

In this work, we report the fabrication of reduced porous organic cage with good water solubility as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Au3+ in aqueous solution. After the reduction of imine bonds, the prepared porous organic cage possesses good water solubility, because of the existence of many hydroxyl groups and secondary amine groups. Owing to the specific redox reaction between the prepared porous organic cage and Au3+, the porous organic cage transformed to dimer, resulting in the change of dual fluorescence emission. On the basis of the dual fluorescence signal, the water-soluble porous organic cage was applied in ratiometric fluorescent sensing of dissociative Au3+ with good sensitivity, selectivity, and accuracy in environmental and biological water samples incubated with gold nanoparticles.

All reagents used are at least of analytical grade. 2-Hydroxyl-1,3,5-benzenetricarbaldehyde (HO-TFB) was bought from Yanshen Technology Co., Ltd. (Jilin, China). (1R,2R)-cyclohexane-1,2-diamine (R,R-1,2-DACH), NaOH, NaBH4, HAuCl4, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, NaAc, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CrCl3, MgSO4, MnCl2, NiSO4, PbCl2, ZnSO4, AlCl3, FeCl3, ZrCl4, AgNO3, HgCl2, Cu(NO3)2, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid, and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dichloromethane (DCM) and ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from Concord Technology (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water was bought from Hangzhou Wahaha Group Co. (Hangzhou, China).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra in KBr were recorded on an IRPrestige-21 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was collected on a Talos F200S transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Pattern of powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a D/max-2500 diffractometer with CuKα radiation (Rigaku, Japan). Hydrodynamic size was determined on a NanoZS Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer fitted with a monochromated Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.6 eV), hybrid (magnetic/electrostatic) optics, and a multichannel plate and delay line detector (Thermo Fisher, UK). Mass spectra (MS) were acquired on an Agilent 7890A/5975C (Agilent, USA). 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (1H NMR) were obtained on an Avance III HD 500 MHz spectrometer with tetramethyl silane (TMS) as an internal standard (Bruker, Switzerland). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2501P spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a PTI QuantaMaster 8000 spectrofluorometer under the excitation at 340 nm with EX/EM slit of 10/10 nm and photomultiplier voltage of 1000 V (Horiba, Canada). Absolute quantum yield was determined on a FLS980 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK). Atomic absorption spectra (AAS) analysis was determined on a TAS-990 spectrophotometer (Persee, Beijing). All fluorescence photos were obtained under the excitation at 365 nm.

The Schiff-base reaction between HO-TFB and R,R-1,2-DACH was used to fabricate CC19R [36,37]. 55 mg of HO-TFB, 60 mg of R,R-1,2-DACH, and 200 μL of 1 M NaOH were dispersed in 10 mL of EtOH. The mixture was then stirred at 70 °C overnight. After centrifugation (15000 rpm, 15 min), washing with EtOH for twice, and further vacuum drying overnight, the yellowish powder of CC19R was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of reduced porous organic cage (RCC19R)
ce:section.ce:para: 100 mg of CC19R was dispersed in 50 mL of H2O with ultrasonication. 100 mg of NaBH4 was then added to the mixture. After stirring at room temperature overnight, the product was extracted with 50 mL of DCM. The yellowish-green powder of RCC19R was then collected by washing with H2O, rotary evaporation, and further vacuum drying overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of Au3+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL of 0.83 mM RCC19R was mixed with 0.5 mL of 40 mM PBS. After the adding of different concentration of Au3+, the mixture was diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water at room temperature. The effects of the concentration of Au3+ ([Au3+]), pH, incubation time, and coexisting ions on the fluorescence spectra and the intensity ratio of fluorescence at 484 nm to that at 420 nm (F484/F420) were then evaluated. All solutions (Au3+, RCC19R, and PBS) and all operation (dilution, incubation, and fluorescence determination) were kept at a certain temperature. Then the F484/F420 was obtained at different temperature to explore the effect of temperature on the sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection, pretreatment, and analysis of environmental and biological water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Domestic wastewater (pH 6) was obtained locally. Human serum (pH 7.4) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China), and was filtrated with Amicon Ultra-4 centrifugal Filter Units (30 kDa) by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 30 min). 0.8 mL of wastewater and human serum were both mixed with 0.2 mL of 2.33 mM citric acid protected gold nanoparticles (CAGNPs) which were synthesized according to Turkevich [38]. After 24-h incubation, the sample solutions were collected after centrifugation (10000 rpm, 5 min). 50 μL of 0.83 mM RCC19R was mixed with 0.5 mL of 40 mM PBS (pH 5). After the adding of 1 mL of wastewater sample or 20 μL of human serum sample, the mixture was diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water. The fluorescence spectra were measured after 20-min incubation.

The fabrication of RCC19R and the ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Au3+ in aqueous solution are illustrated in Scheme 1 . CC19R was first synthesized via Schiff-base reaction in EtOH, and then the imine bonds in CC19R were reduced by NaBH4 to obtain the fluorescent RCC19R with good water solubility. On the basis of the dual fluorescence signal arising from the specific redox reaction and coordination interaction between RCC19R and Au3+, the RCC19R is promising as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Au3+ in aqueous solution.

The prepared CC19R was characterized by 1H NMR and MS, both showing that CC19R was synthesized successfully. The formation of HCN bond was proved by the 1H NMR peaks from 8.69 to 8.04 ppm (Fig. S1) [36]. The m/z of [CC19R+2H]2+ (591.42) and [CC19R+3H]3+ (394.85) were almost same to that calculated of CC19R (Fig. S2) [36]. The XRD peaks of prepared CC19R corresponding to that of simulation, also verified the successful synthesis of CC19R (Fig. S3) [36]. But all the XRD peaks shifted to lower angle slightly, owing to looser packing pattern of the synthesized CC19R [15]. In addition, the strong XRD peaks at 22.464° and 34.135° indicated the main growth of the prepared CC19R along the (026) and (139) crystal plane [36]. The characteristic FTIR peaks of CH2 at 2929 and 2855 cm−1, the peak of CN at 1635 cm−1, and the peak of CC at 1603 cm−1 for CC19R also showed the successful fabrication of CC19R (Fig. 1 A) [36]. After the reduction by NaBH4, the imine bond transformed to secondary amine, which was proved by the absence of FTIR peak of CN at 1635 cm−1 and the presence of the peak of NH at 3295 cm−1 in RCC19R (Fig. 1A). Moreover, the 1H NMR and MS results also verified the successful synthesis of RCC19R. The 1H NMR peaks from 4.58 to 2.74 ppm showed the NCH and NCH2 groups in RCC19R (Fig. S4) [36]. The m/z of [RCC19R+H]+ (1207.12), [RCC19R+2H]2+ (603.58), [RCC19R+3H]3+ (402.81), and [RCC19R+4H]4+ (302.29) were almost same to that calculated of RCC19R (Fig. S5).

The prepared RCC19R showed a significant absorption peak at 284 nm and a weak shoulder absorption peak at 340 nm (Fig. 1B). The RCC19R gave the fluorescence emission at 420 nm with absolute quantum yield of 0.8% at pH 5 under the excitation at 340 nm, which was enough for the sensing application (Fig. 1C). Moreover, owing to the deprotonation of nitrogen and oxygen atoms in the RCC19R, the auxochromic ability of secondary amine and hydroxyl group increased, leading to the obvious fluorescence increase along with the increase of pH from 1 to 5 (Fig. 1D) [13]. In contrast, an almost constant fluorescence intensity was observed in the pH range of 5–7, showing no significant deprotonation (Fig. 1D) [13]. Besides, the fluorescence increased slightly as pH increased from 7 to 10, caused by the further deprotonation (Fig. 1D) [13]. Furthermore, fluorescence of the prepared RCC19R showed good stability in H2O for at least 7 days (Fig. S6). The photo and fluorescence photo of RCC19R/H2O solution were also recorded, revealing the good water solubility and blue fluorescence of RCC19R (Fig. S7). The above results indicate the successful synthesis of fluorescent RCC19R with good water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction between RCC19R and Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: The red solution containing gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) was obtained after the interaction between RCC19R and Au3+, but the solution turned yellow at the Au3+ concentration of 200 μM (Fig. S8). The AuNPs in both red and yellow solution were characterized by TEM and DLS, indicating that Au3+ was reduced by the prepared RCC19R (Figs. 2A and S9). The TEM image of the red solution showed the presence of AuNPs with the diameter of 4.33 ± 0.07 nm and the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which was furtherly proved by the hydrodynamic diameter of the generated red solution of 40.73 ± 2.12 nm. Smaller sized AuNPs with the diameter of 2.34 ± 0.10 nm and the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm in the yellow solution were observed in comparison to AuNPs in red solution. Besides, the TEM image also indicated the aggregation of AuNPs in the yellow solution. The hydrodynamic diameter of yellow solution was 1120.67 ± 84.35 nm, also evidenced the aggregation state of AuNPs in yellow solution.

To investigate the interaction between RCC19R and Au3+ furtherly, the AuNPs were obtained via centrifugation (15000 rpm, 10 min) from the reaction mixture of RCC19R and Au3+. The XPS spectra of AuNPs were also been recorded to investigate the interaction between RCC19R and Au3+. The Au 4f7/2 spectrum was deconvoluted into Au+ (85.2 eV) and Au0 (82.6 eV) components, demonstrating the reduction of Au3+ by RCC19R (Fig. 2 B) [39]. Moreover, the Au+ content was observed to constitute 93.4% of all Au atoms in the AuNPs. The high Au+ content revealed that most of the Au3+ was reduced to Au+, which was stabilized as complexes immediately.

The RCC19R oxidized by Au3+ (OXRCC19R) was eluted from the AuNPs using NaOH/EtOH solution, and was characterized by MS. The m/z of [OXRCC19R+2H]2+ (1206.06) and [OXRCC19R+3H]3+ (603.17) showed that the RCC19R transformed to dimer, proving the redox process during the incubation (Fig. S10). In addition, the 1H NMR result of OXRCC19R indicated the generation of a bit of CN at 8.70–8.00 ppm, revealing the oxidation of a small amount of secondary amine in RCC19R during the incubation (Fig. S11) [36]. The inconspicuous FTIR peak of OXRCC19R at 1633 cm−1 also indicated the formation of a bit of CN after the incubation with Au3+ (Fig. 2C) [36]. The characteristic FTIR peaks at 2929, 2855, and 3295 cm−1 showed the still presence of CH2 and NH in OXRCC19R (Fig. 2C). The appearance of the O–O peak in OXRCC19R at 848 cm−1 also revealed the transformation of RCC19R to dimer during the interaction with Au3+ (Fig. 2C). OXRCC19R exhibited the peak of CC at 1599 cm−1 with a slight shift compared to that of RCC19R at 1603 cm−1, indicating narrower energy band gap of π→π∗ transition in the dimer (Fig. 2C) [40,41]. Besides, the AuNPs separated from the reaction mixture gave similar FTIR peaks to OXRCC19R (Fig. 2C). Due to the charge transfer caused by the coordination between OXRCC19R and AuNPs, the peak of CC at 1590 cm−1 in AuNPs showed a slight shift compared to that of OXRCC19R at 1599 cm−1 (Fig. 2C) [17].

After the interaction with Au3+, the absorption peak of RCC19R at 284 nm remained the same position (Fig. 1B). Besides, the shoulder peak of RCC19R at 340 nm disappeared, in conjugation with the appearance of the absorption peaks at 330, 380, and 580 nm (Fig. 1B). To figure out the origin of new absorption peaks, the UV–vis spectra of AuNPs and supernatant which were obtained via centrifugation were tested. The AuNPs showed a strong absorption peak at 540 nm, corresponding to the wide absorption peak at 580 nm of RCC19R after the interaction with Au3+ (Figs. 1B and S12) [42]. In addition, AuNPs, supernatant, and the RCC19R incubated with Au3+ all exhibited absorption peaks at 330 and 380 nm, which were inherited from OXRCC19R (Figs. 1B and S12). Compared to the RCC19R, the absorption at 380 nm of the OXRCC19R also indicated the narrower energy band gap of π→π∗ transition [40,41].

Owing to the narrower π→π∗ transition, the generated OXRCC19R exhibited yellowish-green fluorescence (Fig. S13). The fluorescence emission red-shifted from 420 nm to 484 nm after the incubation with Au3+ (Fig. 1C). Depending on [Au3+], the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm decreased while the emission at 484 nm increased gradually in the concentration range of 0–60 μM (Fig. 4 A). But the emission at both 420 and 484 nm quenched gradually as the increase of [Au3+] from 60 to 200 μM (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence photo also showed the same change of emission (Fig. S14). Moreover, the AuNPs showed quite low fluorescence, but the supernatant gave strong fluorescence similar to OXRCC19R (Fig. S15).

Thus, the interesting fluorescence change was caused by the redox reaction and coordination interaction between RCC19R and Au3+. In lower concentration range of Au3+, RCC19R and OXRCC19R were sufficient to coordinately protect AuNPs. Because of the ligand-Au+-Au0 charge transfer, the fluorescence of ligands was quenched almost completely by AuNPs. Therefore, the surplus dissociative RCC19R and OXRCC19R in solution were responsible for the strong emission. Along with the increase of [Au3+], the content of RCC19R decreased and that of OXRCC19R increased, leading to the quenching of fluorescence at 420 nm and the enhancement of fluorescence at 484 nm. In higher concentration range of Au3+, the RCC19R was completely consumed, and the generated OXRCC19R was almost entirely used to stabilize the AuNPs, which was accompanied by the fluorescence quenching caused by ligand-Au+-Au0 charge transfer in AuNPs [17].

To verify the effect of the redox reaction between RCC19R and Au3+ on the fluorescence emission furtherly, a common reducing agent AA which can also reduce Au3+, was added to the mixture of RCC19R and 50 μM Au3+. Increase of fluorescence at 420 nm and apparent decease of fluorescence at 484 nm were observed, along with the increase of the concentration of AA ([AA]) from 0 to 50 μM, showing less and less redox process between RCC19R and Au3+ (Fig. S16). The F484/F420 decreased distinctly in the same concentration range, revealing the great importance of the redox reaction between Au3+ and RCC19R to the dual fluorescence change (Fig. 4C). In addition, no obvious change of F484/F420 was observed with different concentration of PBS (Fig. S17). The above results indicate the redox reaction and coordination interaction between the RCC19R and Au3+ in aqueous solution.

As mentioned above, the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm decreased gradually in conjugation with the increase of emission at 484 nm, depending on [Au3+] in 0–60 μM. Thus, the F484/F420 enhanced with the increase of [Au3+], providing great potential of RCC19R as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Au3+ in aqueous solution (Fig. 5 A). Moreover, the redox and coordination interaction between RCC19R and Au3+ exhibited a fast process. The F484/F420 increased quickly before 20 min (Fig. 5B). Therefore, the following experiments were performed with 20-min incubation.

The effect of pH on the F484/F420 of RCC19R with 50 μM Au3+ in aqueous solution was determined. The fluorescence at 484 nm and the F484/F420 increased notably as pH increased from 1 to 5, because of the increased reducing capacity of deprotonated RCC19R to Au3+ (Figs. 5C and S18). Besides, in spite of no obvious deprotonation of RCC19R in the pH range of 5–7, the hydrolysis of Au3+ occurred at higher pH, inhibiting the interaction between RCC19R and Au3+. As a result, the fluorescence at 484 nm and the F484/F420 decreased as pH increased from 5 to 7 (Figs. 5C and S18). In the pH range of 7–10, the hydrolysis of Au3+ became more and more severe, bringing more difficulty to the redox and coordination reaction. The emission at 484 nm and the F484/F420 were very small without no obvious change (Figs. 5C and S18). As a control, the F484/F420 of RCC19R without Au3+ was almost constant in all pH (Fig. 5C). Thus, the highest net ratio of F484/F420 was obtained at pH 5. Moreover, the absorbance of generated OXRCC19R at 330 nm and the integral area of the wide absorption of generated AuNPs also showed the similar variation trend, verifying the change of extent of the redox and coordination reaction between RCC19R and Au3+ along with the change of pH (Figs. S19 and S20).

We examined the change of F484/F420 of RCC19R with [Au3+] at pH 5 in aqueous solution. The F484/F420 linearly increased with [Au3+] from 1 to 60 μM with a calibration function of F484/F420 = 0.0370[Au3+] + 0.5689 ([Au3+] in μM, R2 = 0.9975) (Fig. 5A). According to IUPAC and ICH, we also determined the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) of the proposed method [43,44]. The LOD (3s) of 8 nM and the LOQ (10s) of 28 nM are comparable to or better than those obtained by other organic fluorescent probes for Au3+ (Table S1). The relative standard deviation for 10 replicate detections of 50 μM Au3+ was 0.26%, indicating good precision of the proposed method for Au3+ sensing in aqueous solution.

To explore the effect of water solubility on detection performance of porous organic cage, CC19R with poor water processability was applied to Au3+ sensing. After the incubation with Au3+ for 20 min, the fluorescence spectra changed slightly with a calibration function of F500/F530 = 0.0016[Au3+] + 0.9961, where F500/F530 is the intensity ratio of fluorescence at 500 nm to that at 530 nm ([Au3+] in μM, R2 = 0.9951) in the concentration range of 1–60 μM, showing a much lower sensitivity (Figs. S21 and S22). Moreover, due to the strong precipitation tendency caused by the poor water solubility of CC19R, the fluorescence measurement was so difficult that the precision of CC19R-based sensing was very bad (Figs. S22 and S23).

In consideration of the rapidity of detection, we also evaluated the effect of temperature on the response time of RCC19R-based sensing. After 5-min incubation, the F484/F420 increased as the increase of temperature from 20 to 70 °C, but the F484/F420 changed inconspicuously above 70 °C (Fig. S24). Thus, the variation of F484/F420 was measured with different incubation time at 70 °C (Fig. S25). The F484/F420 showed no obvious variation after 10 min. Therefore, the Au3+ sensing was accomplished with 10-min incubation at 70 °C. The F484/F420 linearly increased with a calibration function of F484/F420 = 0.0447[Au3+] + 0.8344 ([Au3+] in μM, R2 = 0.9963) in the concentration range of 0.1–60 μM and the LOD (3s) of 7 nM (Fig. S26). In comparison with the Au3+ sensing at room temperature, in spite of the little faster sensing showed slightly better sensitivity, the maintaining at 70 °C during the sensing process was much more complicated.

The selectivity of the prepared RCC19R for the ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Au3+ in aqueous solution was investigated. Owing to the weaker oxidation ability of other ions than that of Au3+, other ions showed less influence on F484/F420 of RCC19R (Fig. S27). All changes of F484/F420 resulted from the coordination between RCC19R and other ions. Furthermore, the effect of coexisting ions on the F484/F420 of RCC19R in the presence of 50 μM Au3+ was tested. For comparison, the F484/F420 in the presence of coexisting ions was then normalized to that only in 50 μM Au3+. Even the concentration was 10 times that of Au3+, coexisting ions (Ac−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Hg2+) showed no significant interference to the ratiometric sensing (Fig. 5D). Besides, the disturbances of 500 μM Fe3+, Cu2+, Zr4+, Pb2+, and Ag+ to the Au3+ sensing caused by their stronger competition coordination ability than that of other coexisting ions, were eliminated by 500 μM EDTA (Fig. 5D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in environmental and biological water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical utility of the proposed method, we applied the RCC19R to detect Au3+ in environmental and biological water samples. Before the incubation with CAGNPs, the ultrafiltration for human serum was necessary to minimize the influence of macromolecules such as fluorescent proteins on the following fluorescent sensing. Besides, the domestic wastewater and human serum were analyzed by AAS, and no Au3+ in these samples before the incubation with CAGNPs was detected (Table 1 ). The CAGNPs precipitated gradually during the incubation. After the incubation, the residual CAGNPs were removed via centrifugation to decrease their interference to the following fluorescence determination. Because of the extremely low concentration of coexisting ions in domestic wastewater and human serum samples, such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Zr4+, Pb2+, and Ag+, the real water samples were not pretreated with EDTA after the incubation with CAGNPs [45].

The recoveries of spiked Au3+ in these samples ranged from 94.66% to 105.61%, indicating no significant interferences in the detection of Au3+ in wastewater and human serum samples (Table 1). The concentrations of Au3+ in wastewater and human serum samples determined by the proposed method were in agreement with those measured by AAS (Table 1). The above results indicate good selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy of the proposed simple method for quantitative detection of Au3+ in environmental and biological water samples.

In summary, we have prepared fluorescent RCC19R with good water solubility for sensitive and selective sensing of Au3+ in aqueous solution. In view of the change of dual fluorescence signal resulting from the specific redox interaction between RCC19R and Au3+, we have further applied RCC19R as a simple ratiometric fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of Au3+ generated from the dissociation of gold nanoparticles with good selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy in environmental water and biological fluids. The proposed sensing method has emphasized the great potential of porous organic cage with good water solubility in fluorescence sensing.

Cong Dai: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Biao Gu: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Si-Ping Tang: Investigation. Pei-Hong Deng: Investigation. Bo Liu: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Youth Program of Nature Science Foundation of Hunan Province [grant number 2020JJ5008]; A Project Supported by Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department [grant number 20C0298]; and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 21907026].
